message:
test(ft-conformance): subprocess libm conformance for trig family

Locks the entire f64 trig surface (sin / cos / tan / asin / acos /
atan) against the platform libm via ctypes-libm subprocess oracle.

Rust's `f64::sin / cos / tan / asin / acos / atan` FFI directly to
the platform libm via libstd (glibc on Linux runners — distinct
from the Rust `libm` crate, which is the MUSL-derived port that
underlies the erf/erfc/lgamma/erfinv harnesses where 2-16 ULP
tolerance was required for libm-impl drift). PyTorch's `torch.sin`
etc. wrap the SAME platform libm via the same FFI path, so this
oracle should match Rust f64 stdlib bit-for-bit on the entire f64
domain.

A single test covers six libm calls per input rather than one
harness per op, exercising:
  * Trivial / signed zero (0, ±0).
  * Exact small values (±0.1, ±0.5, ±1.0).
  * π and rational multiples (PI, FRAC_PI_2, FRAC_PI_3, FRAC_PI_4,
    FRAC_PI_6, FRAC_PI_8, 2*PI) — the canonical zero / ±1 anchors
    for sin / cos.
  * Mid-range generic (±0.7, ±1.5, ±2.5).
  * Subnormals + smallest positive (f64::MIN_POSITIVE, 5e-324, 1e-15).
  * Large arguments (1e6, 1e10, 1e16, 1e18) — argument-reduction
    drift surfaces here as multi-ULP error.
  * Transcendental constants (E, LN_2, SQRT_2, FRAC_1_SQRT_2).

For asin / acos the matrix is restricted to |x| <= 1 (libm produces
NaN out-of-domain and the test would just be NaN-equivalence). For
atan it's the trig matrix plus ±inf and NaN.

Tolerance: 1 ULP (with NaN-payload-equivalence). Both sides hit
glibc through equivalent FFI paths so bit-exact is the expected
outcome; 1 ULP is a defense against any future libstd-internal
libm variant change.

This is a "regression detector" harness — passes today and locks
the parity. Any future replacement of a Rust f64 trig method with
a hand-rolled approximation (the same class of regression we hit on
erf and lgamma where the in-tree code drifted to single-precision
quality) surfaces immediately as a many-ULP test failure.

Quality gates: ft-conformance 101 tests pass; clippy + ubs clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
